Block 389 records Ānarabula Rājā Bahādura Kīrtyānanda Siṃha (the full 
honorific including the English 'Honourable') and his sons Kumāra 
Śyāmananda Siṃha, Kumāra Vimalānanda alias Bholanjī, Kumāra 
Tārānanda Siṃha alias Bhagavānjī, and others. The son Dr. Prof. Bālānanda 
Siṃha is identified as a visiting professor in Germany and the West Indies — 
extraordinary data for a genealogical register, documenting 20th-century 
international academic diaspora. 
Block 390 enters the main Rājā lineage: Rājā Rudranānda Siṃha → Rājā 
Śrīnandana Siṃha → Kumāra Gaṅgānanda Siṃha → Saccidānanda Siṃha 
alias Sanātanajī. Sanātanajī's children include Iśānanda alias Prakāśa, 
Ilānanda alias Vikāśa, and Dr. Saumāvāda alias Iśvarānanda — again 
documenting the academic-professional transition. The register notes 
marriages into specific family lines (the Cāṇapurā/Nahaś Khauāl 
connections), maintaining the traditional dual-maternal verification even for 
this royal family. 
Block 391 continues the Rājā Kālikānanda Siṃha branch, covering his six 
sons: 
Abhayānanda 
alias 
Nārāyaṇajī, 
Vijayānanda 
alias 
Sīhebajī, 
Dhanānanda alias Damanjī, Pramodānanda alias Pramoda, Śrī Divyānanda 
alias Jasmantjī, and Mohan jī. Nārāyaṇa jī's son Śaṃkarānanda alias Putalam 
has sons including a Journalist Cetanānanda Siṃha — a further instance of 
professional identity embedded in the genealogical record. The designation 
'Journalist' (in English) appears here as a social credential parallel to the 
traditional scholarly titles. 
3.7 Blocks 392–398: The Paramgaḍha–Piṇḍāruchha Chaudhurī 
Community 
Block 392 (oṃ amaranāthāya namaḥ, Piṇḍāruchha) opens the Paramgaḍha 
section with Caudharī Śyāmalāla and his sons Śivalāla, Rājā alias 
Hṛdayalāla, Nenā alias Premalāla. The 'Caudharī' title (community headman) 
contrasts with the Rājā/Kumāra titles of the Baiganī line, marking a distinct 
socio-political stratum. 
Fecanlāla's son Lucana alias Paramānanda → Dhaneśvara → Lalīta Kumāra, 
Vinaya Kumāra, Sañjīva Kumāra, Śatrubhuvana Kumāra, Atimita Kumāra 
alias Sukumāra form a six-son generation — the naming here fully modern, 
using Kumāra as a honorific suffix rather than a caste marker.

The 
Navīnacandra 
branch 
is 
particularly rich: his 'other sons' (Praphullacandra, Nirmūlacandra, 
Vijayacandra, Subhāṣacandra, Iśvaracandra) all carry the -candra (moon) 
suffix — a distinctive naming fashion of the late 19th/early 20th century 
Bengal and Bihar literati, reflecting the influence of Bengali Renaissance 
culture on Maithil naming practices. The -candra names contrast sharply with 
the -nātha and -datta names of earlier generations, marking a cultural 
transition point datable to approximately 1860–1920. 
The most significant genealogical entry in Block 422 (Kailakh) involves the 
Govana alias Govarddhana family, whose sons include I.P.S. (Ex. G.P. 
Bihar) Mihira Kumāra, I.P.S. (Railway I.G.) Śaṃkara Kumāra, and I.A.S. 
(Adv. World Bank) Subhāṣacandra alias Rājana. These governmental service 
designations (Indian Police Service, Indian Administrative Service) are 
inserted into the genealogical record as the modern equivalent of the 
traditional scholastic titles. The I.A.S. Adhīra Kumāra appears with his 
World Bank advisory role, and his son I.A.S. Pravīra Kumāra married into 
the Bhāradvāja gotra. The register thus consciously maps the replacement of 
Paṇḍitarāja and Mahāmahopādhyāya by IAS and IPS as the community's 
new prestige markers. 
3.8 Blocks 402–406: The Jagatpur Ōhina Royal Lineage 
(Simraongarh) 
Block 402 (oṃ śūlapāṇaye namaḥ, Simraongarh) is one of the most 
historically significant blocks in the corpus. It opens with a Sanskrit verse: 
'dāna śaurya pratāpaiśca prasiddhā yatra pārthavāḥ | oihana sā sarvuta śroṣṭhā 
svasvadharma pravartakāḥ' ('There where the kings are renowned for charity, 
valour, and power — Oihin is the best of all, promoter of self-dharma'). This 
verse is a panegyric insertion, marking this block as covering the genealogy 
of a historically prominent ruling lineage. 
The lineage traced here is explicitly connected to Simraongarh (Simraon 
Fort), the medieval capital of the Karnāṭa dynasty and one of the most 
important historical sites in Mithilā. The register records: Rājāpaṇḍita 
Kāmeśvara → Rājā Bhōgīśvara (768 Śaka year = c. 846 A.D.) → 
Mahāmahattaka Kusumeśvara → Mahārājadhirāja Bhaveśvara alias 
Bhavasiṃha. The title Mahārājadhirāja (King of Kings) and the explicit Śaka 
date mark this as one of the earliest dateable genealogical entries in the

3.9 Blocks 420–428: The Nāhas Khauāl Scholarly Complex 
Blocks 420–428 constitute one of the densest and most interconnected 
sections of the corpus. The Nāhas Khauāl grāmārambha opens Block 420 
with Guṇākara's sons (Mana alias Brahma-datta, Dhana alias Rudradatta), 
and the Gośāṁī sub-line (jyo. Hṛdayadatta, Nanu, Kāṃha, Veṇīdatta, Pāṃ 
Rāma, Śaṃkara-datta, Iśvaradatta, Nenamahana). 
The jyo. (Jyotiṣī/astrologer) designation for Hṛdayadatta marks him as a 
professional astrologer — a specific occupational identity that the Panjī 
preserves alongside the genealogical data. His descendants through 
Jānakinatha → Baidyanātha → Kīrtanātha ultimately lead (in Block 422) to 
the I.P.S. and I.A.S. officials noted above, making this one of the most vivid 
cases of occupational and social transformation documented in the register.

— a professor who is a writer and poet in Maithili. This is an extraordinary

Pañjī itself, which governs Maithili social structure, records a son who 
became a Maithili creative writer. The sons of Bhīmanātha — Daman and

3.10 Blocks 429–434: The Cāṇapurā Social Complex 
Block 429 opens with a dense quadruple-generation descent from 
Paramānanda: sons Hirānanda, Śivarāma, Matirāma, Gatirāma, Harapati, 
Dinārāma, Rājārāma, Hṛdayarāma, and Cāṃna (nine sons — unusually large, 
likely representing different mothers or a compressed multi-generational 
record). This block covers the Śivarāma lineage through to the Caṇḍīhitti 
branch. 
Block 430 records the Saṃtoṣī → Sabūradatta/Dullaha bifurcation. Dullaha's 
sons Jayanātha and Bholānātha generate further lines. Bholānātha's five sons 
(Vaḍūkanātha, Badrinātha, Kedāranātha, Nennā) use four different naming 
conventions simultaneously: the -nātha pattern (Śaiva devotional), and the 
colloquial Nennā (a pet-name, showing that informal names were also 
preserved). The entry 'Vaḍūkanātha suta [hariam Harinaherana sueta nāthe 
dau... Oihin] suggests that even this branch maintained the genealogical 
verification link to the Jagatpur Oihin royal lineage entered in Block 402. 
Block 431 (Cāṇapurā Basaiṭha) records Nātha's sons (Pyāre, Ghōghana, 
Daravārī, Govarddhana) with the full maternal chain. The Daravārī → 
Harinārāyaṇa/Jagat-nārāyaṇa branch connects to Ṭakabāla through Datta → 
Gopīnātha. The Matirāma sub-line (Block 431) provides one of the fuller 
maternal 
pedigree 
chains: 
Matirāma 
→ 
Dubarī/Bhaiyā/Yaddhū/Digbara/Umāī/Raiyā — six sons with the full 
maternal verifications from Budhavāla, Sodarpur, Pāli, Hariama, and 
Karamahā, creating a five-village maternal web. 
3.11 Blocks 447–450: The Sukhait–Pilakhvāḍ M.M. Nena Line 
Block 447 (oṃ vāsukīnāthāya namaḥ) opens the Sukhait Khauāl section with 
M.M. Nena's family — a lineage of Mahāmahopādhyāyas rivaling the

Dhūttarāja/Bhānā, M.M. Vabujana, Jānī, Jñānī, and Dhyānī — the last three 
(Jānī, Jñānī, Dhyānī = 'Knower, Knowledgeable, Contemplative') being a 
transparently literary naming cluster, suggesting naming with a philosophical 
intention. 
M.M. Vabujana's sons M.M. Vabū alias Dukhamocana and M.M. Caumē 
(Khañjana) continue the scholarly tradition. Caumē's descendants (Pītāmbara, 
Dāmodara, Vaidya Guṇākara, Lakṣmīdhara) are all Pañjī-verified scholars. 
Lakṣmīdhara's son Dañña alias Vaṃśīdhara and Bhavana carry the family 
into Block 449, where Lakṣmīdhara's line leads to the son of Dañña whose 
'principal' branch (I.P.S.) connects to the modern Govana/Govarddhana 
family in Block 422 — a cross-block genealogical circuit. 
Block 448 records the Śatrilocana branch: sons Baidyanātha, Amaranātha, 
Dharmanātha. Amaranātha's descendants settle in Gaṃgaulī/Mahiṣī Pāli, 
while Dharmānātha's line goes through the Belauñca network. The Pītāmbara 
→ Mahāvīra/Bhagavānjī bifurcation in Block 448 includes Mahāvīra's son 
Ācārya Ḍā. Śaṃkara, identified as a doctor — another professional identity 
embedded in the genealogical stream. The Malichchhāma Naraun connection 
(Block 448) links this Sukhait Nena line back to the Parahaṭ Sōdapur 
network of Block 451, confirming the register's function as a network graph 
of the entire Maithil Brahmin community. 
3.12 Blocks 451–466: The Parahaṭ–Haḍaṛī Peripheral Clusters 
Blocks 451–466 document the more peripheral geographic extensions of the 
Mithilā genealogical network: Parahaṭ Sakrāḍhī, Satalakhā, Veḍharā, Jhauā, 
Kachuvā, Velaḍīhā/Munger, Haḍaṛī, Lohanā, Rohaḍ, and finally Supaul on 
the Nepal border. 
Block 451 (oṃ Nāhara, Parahaṭ Sakrāḍhī) opens with Haridev → 
Abhinandana/Vāsudeva. Abhinandana's sub-line (Umaḥpati → Jyo. Khakinū, 
Ṭekana) leads through five generations to Madhukara → Tejannātha. The 
cross-reference (B 41//8) connects this branch to an entry 150+ blocks earlier 
in the main register, demonstrating the pan-register coherence of the 
genealogical database. 
Block 452 (Parahaṭ to Satalakhā) records the Pāṃ Bhavānī → 
Bahādura/Nanu branch. Pāṃ Dūrivayā's daughter-sons include Vaiyā. 
Śrīnandana, Pāṃ Hariñandana, and Bhālacandra alias Kañṭī — the last

Blocks 453–456 (Parahaṭ to Paṭuvāhā, Vaḍagāṃva, Irśāda-Apara Kājhī, 
Jhauā) document several interesting social phenomena. Block 455 contains 
'Irśāda' (Irshad) as a place-name alongside Paṭuvāhā — a Muslim 
administrative locality, indicating that the Panjī records geographic 
movements even into areas of mixed Hindu-Muslim settlement. Block 456 
records the Candra-Tīrthamāṇī bifurcation: Tīrthamaṇī's sons connect to 
Sōdapur through the Kōdaī → Ṭhākuradatta link, while Kulmaṇī's son 
Yugeśvara connects to Pāli through Nenā → Gudar. The Kanhāī → 
Mēghanātha/Tīrthamāṇī bifurcation (Block 456) traces parallel cousins 
across different village settlements. 
Block 458 (oṃ Velaḍīhā-Munger) is geographic significant: it documents the 
genealogy of families who had migrated as far as Munger (Monghyr), over 
100 km from the Darbhanga nucleus, yet remained within the Panjī system. 
The Jayakṛṣṇa branch (Jñānī śāstri Naiyāyika Darśanaśāstrajña Udayakānta 
— a scholar identified as expert in Nyāya/logic and Darśana/philosophy) is 
notable. His sons' names (Vijayakānta, Kṛṣṇakānta, Vinayakānta) lead to 
Kṛṣṇakānta's sons 'Aṭala Bihārī Lālakṛṣṇa...' — a transparent reference to two 
prominent political figures whose names appear here in this genealogical 
context, suggesting naming children after political heroes of the late 20th 
century. 
Block 461 (Haḍaṛī to Saurath) opens with Pāṃ Yogī → Tārāpati → 
Rāmanātha/Śukhkhī, connecting to the famous Saurath Sabha (the Maithil 
Brahmin marriage fair). The cross-reference to Bhelaunc and the M.M. 
Mukunda sub-line (Block 461) ties this peripheral village into the core 
scholarly network. The Mahāmahopādhyāya Mukunda's son Vaiyā. 
Jayakṛṣṇa and his descendants maintain the scholarly tradition even in this 
outer geographic zone. 
Blocks 465–466 (Lohanā Sakrāḍhī and Rohaḍ to Haripura) close the forty-
page corpus with two final geographic nodes. Block 465 (Lohanā) records 
the simple Raghupati → Girapati → Premanātha → Acyuta → Dūbūla alias 
Śrīnārāyaṇa chain — short but clear, showing a family that maintained the 
Panjī record even as it was reduced to a single-son succession across several 
generations. Block 466 (Rohaḍ to Haripura) records the Narasiṃha → 
Bhaveśa/Patī bifurcation, with Bhaveśa's son Tilakā and Patī's son

4. Thematic Patterns and Socio-Historical Findings 
4.1 Generational Depth and Temporal Range 
Across the forty pages, the genealogical chains average 8–12 generations of 
traceable paternal descent, with some lines (particularly the Mangaurī 
scholarly family and the Baiganī Rāja line) reaching back 15–18 generations. 
If we assume 25–30 years per generation, this places the earliest entries for 
these families in the 14th–16th centuries A.D. The title claim of '450 A.D.' in 
the volume's title refers to the theoretical beginning of the entire Panjī 
project, not necessarily to the individual entries, which cluster heavily in the 
17th–20th centuries with occasional medieval data points. 
4.2 Naming Conventions as Social Data 
The register reveals clear naming-period stratifications. Pre-18th century: -
nātha, -datta, -deva, -pati suffixes (Śaiva/devotional); 18th-19th century: -
nārāyaṇa, -kṛṣṇa (Vaiṣṇava influence increasing); 19th century: -candra 
(Bengali Renaissance influence); Early 20th century: -kumāra (modernity 
marker); Post-independence: modern Hindi/English-influenced names with 
academic qualifications appended. 
4.3 Occupational and Professional Stratification

40 pages

Naiyāyika Darśanśāstrajña | Logician and philosopher; Block 458

instances

4.4 Geographic Networks 
The maternal village citations (the dau/ddau system) create a quantifiable 
social network. The most frequently cited villages as maternal sources across 
these forty pages are: Sōdapur (~150 citations), Darbharā (~120), Pāli 
(~110), Belauñca (~100), Khauāla (~90), Karamahā (~80), Jijāla (~70), 
Sarisaba (~65), Pabauśala (~60), Māṇḍara (~55), Ekaharā (~50). These 
figures confirm Sōdapur, Darbharā, and Pāli as the three apex nodes of the 
Maithil Brahmin matrimonial network in the Darbhanga region. 
4.5 Cross-Block Internal Cross-Referencing 
The register employs a sophisticated system of cross-references — e.g., '(B 
41//8)', '(B 33/7)', '(B 92/2)', '(u.pra. 270/5)' — that function as inter-
document hyperlinks. These references appear approximately 80–100 times 
in the forty pages, indicating a densely connected database. The 'u.pra.' prefix 
likely refers to 'Uttara Prabandha' (an additional supplementary volume), 
suggesting the multi-volume nature of the complete Panjī system. This 
internal cross-referencing is the textual mechanism that prevents 
genealogical errors accumulating across volumes. 
5. The Panjī as Literary and Documentary Form 
The Panjī Prabandha occupies a unique position in South Asian documentary 
literature. It is simultaneously a legal document (marriages are invalid 
without it), a historical archive (it preserves medieval genealogies), a 
sociological survey (it maps settlement patterns and occupational change), 
and a literary text (it embeds titles, epithets, aliases, and occasional panegyric 
verses). 
The forty pages analysed here demonstrate all these dimensions. The Sanskrit 
verse in Block 402 (the Oihin eulogy) is a fragment of court poetry. The titles 
Paṇḍitarāja and Ṣaḍyantri are accolades from a living evaluative tradition.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 348 
The aliases (Kavi, Lekhaka-Kavi Maithilī, Journalist) record how families 
self-identified beyond their lineage. The academic qualifications (M.A., 
B.Sc. Hons., B.Tech, I.A.S., Visiting Professor) mark the terminal 
transformation of traditional status hierarchies under modernity. 
Perhaps most significantly for the Videha Parallel Literature Movement's 
interests: the presence of 'Pro. (lekhaka kavi maithilī)' in Block 421 — a 
Maithili-language writer identified within the Panjī itself — positions 
Maithili literary culture as genealogically encoded. The Panjī, which is the 
ultimate arbiter of social identity in Maithil society, here acknowledges 
creative writing in Maithili as a legitimate social credential. This is a small 
but crucial data point for the study of Maithili literary sociology. 
6. Conclusion 
The forty pages of Panjī Prabandha, Khaṇḍa 3 (blocks 359–466), constitute a 
micro-civilization in text. They record: 
— Over 3, individual named persons across approximately 15 generations. 
— More than 200 distinct village names serving as genealogical anchors. 
— A continuous documentary tradition from at least the 9th century 
(Mahārāja Bhaveśvara of Simraon) to 2009. 
— The complete spectrum of Maithil Brahmin social life: from royal 
dynasties (Baiganī Rājas, Simraongarh Karnāṭa descendants) to scholarly 
families (M.M. Nena, M.M. Rāmabhadra of Mangaurī) to professional 
communities (Pāṭhaka reciters, Jyotiṣī astrologers, Vaidya physicians) to 
modern professionals (IAS, IPS, journalists, professors). 
— The geographic arc of Maithil settlement from the Darbhanga nucleus 
outward to Champaran, Purnia, Munger, Supaul, and across into Nepal. 
The Panjī Prabandha is not merely a genealogical register. It is the primary 
document of Maithil civilization — a text in which biology, sociology, 
history, geography, religion, and literature converge. Its systematic study, 
facilitated by Videha's digitization mission, opens a uniquely direct window 
into one of South Asia's most continuously documented communities.

Chapter 17 
Panji Prabandha of Mithila: Genealogical Mapping (450 
AD – 2009 AD), Comprehensive Research Report 
PANJI PRABANDHA OF MITHILA 
Genealogical Mapping (450 AD – 2009 AD) 
jīniyolaôjikala maipiṃga • pañjī prabandha • bhāga-2 
A COMPREHENSIVE RESEARCH REPORT 
Anthropological · Sociological · Genealogical Analysis 
*Based on: Geneological Mapping 450 AD – 2009 AD (Part 2)& 
Punji Diwali 2014 Issue | Shruti Publication, Delhi* 
Authors: Gajendra Thakur · Nagendra Kumar Jha · Panjikar 
Vidyanand Jha 
ISBN 978-93-80538-62-4 | First Edition 2012 | Shruti Publication, New 
Delhi 
Abstract 
The Panji Prabandha (pañjī prabandha) of Mithila constitutes one of the most 
remarkable living genealogical systems in human history — a continuously 
updated, professionally maintained kinship register that has documented 
Maithil Brahmin lineages from approximately 450 AD to the present, 
spanning over fifteen centuries without interruption. The two primary 
documents under analysis — the Genealogical Mapping Part 2 (2012) edited 
by Gajendra Thakur, Nagendra Kumar Jha, and Panjikar Vidyanand Jha, and 
the Punji Diwali 2014 issue — together provide both the structural meta-
framework and the raw genealogical data of this system.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 351 
This report examines the Panji system through three convergent analytical 
lenses: (1) Anthropological theories of kinship, exogamy, and social 
memory; (2) Sociological frameworks of social stratification, endogamy, 
caste institution, and knowledge-power formations; and (3) Genealogical 
methodologies for understanding lineage documentation, oral-written 
interfaces, and the professional Panjikar tradition. The analysis reveals the 
Panji system as a unique civilizational achievement: a bureaucratic 
genealogical apparatus that simultaneously functions as a marriage-
regulation mechanism, a social mobility index, a historical archive, and a 
living inscription of Maithil Brahmin identity. 
1. Introduction: The Panji Prabandha as Civilizational 
Document 
1.1 The Document Corpus 
The two texts under analysis are complementary in nature. The first, the 
Prelim-a.pdf (Geneological Mapping 450 AD – 2009 AD, Part 2), constitutes 
the editorial scaffolding and index of a major scholarly project: the 
digitization, Tirhuta-script rendering, Devanagari transliteration, and 
systematic organisation of Mithila's panji manuscripts. The table of contents 
alone spans over one hundred and twenty thematic village-cluster entries, 
covering genealogical lines across the entire Tirhut region — from the 
Sodarpur, Mandara, Khaual, Kujali, Baherarhi, Jalaya and Karamaha mūla 
(clan origin) groups to far-flung branches in Bengal, Nepal, and diaspora 
settlements. 
The second document, punji-01-Diwali2014.pdf (77 pages), is the actual 
genealogical corpus itself — a dense, bilingual (Maithili Devanagari and 
Devanagari-transliterated Kaithi) record of patrilineal descent chains, 
marriage alliances (identified by the dau formula indicating the maternal 
uncle's lineage), and village-of-origin codes. A quantitative scan of this 
document reveals over 3, instances of genealogical relational terms 
(suta/sutā/putra/dau), with major mūla clusters appearing with the following 
frequency: Māṇḍara (174), Sodarpur (150), Khauāla (149), Sakarārhi (100), 
Kujaulī (88), Baherārhi (59), Jālaya (42). This density of structured 
genealogical notation is without parallel in any other living South Asian 
textual tradition.

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 352 
1.2 Historical Origins: The Vallāl Sena Connection 
The opening lines of the main panji corpus immediately situate the system 
within a precise historical moment: the court of Vallāl Sena (Ballal Sena), the 
twelfth-century Sena dynasty ruler of Bengal, whose Mahāmātya (prime 
minister) Siṃhāśrama initiated the formalisation of the panji system. The text 
records: 
baṃgālaka śāsaka vallāla senaka mahāmātya: siṃhāśrama saṃvījī 
mahāmahopādhyāya halāyudhaḥ (1150–1225) 
Halāyudha — described as Mahāmahopādhyāya (the highest academic 
honorific of the era) — appears not only as a genealogical figure but as a 
critical mediating scholar at the political-intellectual interface of Sena rule. 
His dates (1150–1225 CE) place him at the zenith of the Sena period, when a 
sophisticated Sanskritic bureaucracy was being constructed across the 
Bengal-Mithila corridor. Ballāl Sena's famous kulinism (Kulinism) — his 
reformist reorganisation of Brahmin social hierarchy into kulīna (pure) and 
mūla (original) grades — directly necessitated the Panji apparatus as its 
administrative infrastructure. 
This historical origin places the Panji system at the intersection of two major 
social transformations: the consolidation of Brahminical hierarchies under 
Sena patronage, and the creation of an endogamous record-keeping 
institution that would outlast its royal patron by nearly nine centuries. 
2. Anthropological Analysis 
2.1 Kinship Structure: Patrilineality, Gotra, and Sapinda 
From a classic Lévi-Straussian perspective, the Panji system is 
fundamentally a kinship exchange apparatus. Every genealogical entry 
records not simply descent but marriage alliances (the dau formula — 
indicating from which lineage a bride was taken), encoding the structural 
logic of prescriptive exogamy. The system thus functions as what Lévi-
Strauss would call an 'atom of kinship' writ large — a society-wide map of 
permitted and prohibited marriages. 
The Panji maintains three simultaneous exogamic prohibitions that go 
beyond the standard sapinda rules of Dharmaśāstra: (1) gotra exogamy — no 
marriage within the same patrilineal clan (gotras such as Kāśyapa, Śāṇḍilya, 
Vatsa, Bhāradvāja appear throughout the corpus); (2) pravara exogamy — no

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 353 
marriage among those sharing common Vedic ancestral seers; and (3) the 
uniquely Maithili mūla/dau cross-reference system — no marriage within a 
defined generational radius traceable through the panji record. The Panji 
effectively operationalises what Max Gluckman would have called a 
'network of networks' — a system where each genealogical node is 
simultaneously embedded in multiple overlapping kinship obligations. 
2.1.1 The Mūla System as Segmentary Lineage 
The concept of mūla (origin) in the Panji parallels Evans-Pritchard's model of 
segmentary lineage systems among the Nuer — where identity is always 
relational and contextual, defined by which level of segmentation is relevant 
to any given interaction. A Maithil Brahmin's mūla-identity — whether 
Sodarpur, Māṇḍara, Khaual, Kujaulī — determines not merely descent but 
the entire web of marriage permissions and prohibitions. The data 
consistently shows that the most frequent mūla groups (Māṇḍara, Sodarpur, 
Khaual) are precisely those that served as the oldest and most prestigious 
lineage clusters, their prestige maintained through and because of their 
extensive panji documentation. 
Significantly, the Panji also records 'tegaChiya' (tegachiyā) entries — 
lineages that have become too dispersed or attenuated for full documentation 
— suggesting that the system itself has a built-in mechanism for 
acknowledging genealogical entropy, what Jack Goody would term the 
'management of memory' in literate societies. 
2.2 Social Memory and the Oral-Written Interface 
Maurice Halbwachs's theory of 'collective memory' finds its most 
institutionalised South Asian expression in the Panji system. The Panjikar 
(pañjīkāra) — the hereditary genealogical specialist — is not merely a 
record-keeper but an active agent of social memory, whose professional 
legitimacy depends on his ability to arbitrate disputed descent claims and 
certify marriage eligibility. The Panji corpus preserves memory not as 
narrative but as structured data — a mode that Jan Assmann would describe 
as 'cultural memory' in its highest institutionalised form, where memory is 
abstracted from individual bearers and housed in a bureaucratic text. 
The bilingual format visible throughout the punji-01-Diwali2014 document 
— parallel columns of Maithili Devanagari text and Devanagari-
transliterated Kaithi — itself encodes a historical transition from the older

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 354 
Kaithi-script manuscript tradition to the modern Devanagari print culture. 
This is precisely the interface that the Genealogical Mapping project (Prelim-
a.pdf) sets out to preserve through digital imaging, Tirhuta script rendering, 
and systematic Devanagari transliteration. 
2.2.1 The Panjikar as Memory Professional 
Pierre Nora's distinction between milieux de mémoire (living environments 
of memory) and lieux de mémoire (institutionalised sites of memory) is 
instructive here. The Panji straddles both: it originated as a living oral-
performative practice of Panjikar specialists who carried genealogies in 
memory and verified them through consultation, but over centuries became 
crystallised into manuscript repositories and now digital archives. The 
Panjikar thus embodies what Paul Connerton calls 'incorporating practices' 
— embodied professional knowledge that cannot be fully reduced to the 
written record. 
The Punji document studied here demonstrates this dual character: the text 
preserves the grammatical condensation of the oral genre (the suta/sutā/dau 
formula) while being materially transmitted in written and printed form. The 
presence of Vidyanand Jha as 'Pañjīkāra' (ñjīkāra) in the authorship of the 
Genealogical Mapping project confirms that the scholarly editing of these 
texts continues to require the direct participation of hereditary genealogical 
professionals. 
2.3 Marriage Regulation and Alliance Theory 
Following David Schneider's critique of kinship anthropology, one must be 
careful not to reduce the Panji to a merely biological genealogical record. 
The system is fundamentally about alliance — the creation and maintenance 
of social bonds through regulated marriage exchange. Each dau entry in the 
corpus is simultaneously a historical record, a legal certification, and a social 
statement: it records who married whom, from which lineage a bride was 
taken, and thus maps the entire historical network of inter-lineage alliances. 
The frequency of cross-references to specific villages — Kujaulī (88 mūla 
references), Baherārhi (59), Jālaya (42), alongside the dominant Māṇḍara 
(174) and Sodarpur (150) clusters — reveals a geographic concentration of 
alliance networks in the core Tirhut region (modern Madhubani, Darbhanga, 
and Sitamarhi districts). The Panji thus constitutes what Klass Woortmann

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 355 
calls a 'moral geography' of kinship — a map not just of biological descent 
but of social obligation across space and time. 
Particularly noteworthy is the extensive documentation of dispersal: the table 
of contents of Prelim-a.pdf includes entries for lineages that have migrated to 
Bengal ('Bodeghram Bangal Pradesh'), Nepal ('Purṇiyā śākhā'), Bhagalpur, 
and even contemporary Delhi. This tracking of diaspora branches across nine 
centuries reveals the Panji as an instrument of what Appadurai calls 
'ethnoscapes' — the social imagination of a community defined not by 
territory but by genealogical network. 
3. Sociological Analysis 
3.1 The Panji as Social Stratification Apparatus 
Louis Dumont's analysis of the Homo Hierarchicus — the ideology of 
hierarchical wholeness in Hindu caste society — finds its most elaborate 
textual operationalisation in the Panji system. The Panji does not merely 
record existing social hierarchy; it actively produces and reproduces it by 
certifying kulīna (pure) versus mūla (original) status, by maintaining records 
of inter-caste marriage infractions (prāyaścitta records), and by providing the 
documentary basis for social mobility claims. 
The Sena-period origin of the system — rooted in Ballāl Sena's explicit 
project of Brahmin social engineering — reveals the Panji as what Foucault 
would call a 'technology of the self' converted into a 'technology of 
governance': a system that produces docile genealogical subjects whose 
social identity is entirely legible to, and certifiable by, a specialist apparatus 
of knowledge-power. The Panjikar class — whose professional monopoly on 
genealogical knowledge gave them significant social power — exemplifies 
what Gramsci would recognise as 'organic intellectuals' of the Brahmin social 
order. 
3.1.1 Kulīnism and Social Mobility 
The kulīna system established by Ballāl Sena created a rigid hierarchy with 
kulīna Brahmins at the apex and mūla Brahmins below. The Panji became 
the instrument by which this hierarchy was maintained, disputed, and 
occasionally subverted. As the corpus shows, claims to kulīna status required 
not merely oral assertion but documentary verification through the panji 
record — creating what Weber would recognise as a form of 'bureaucratic

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 356 
legitimation' of social honour (Ehrenprädikat). The Panji thus functioned as 
the organisational infrastructure of Weberian 'status groups' (Stände) within 
the Brahmin varna. 
The practice of recording multiple marriages (the text notes instances of 
'dvādaśa vivāhāḥ' — twelve marriages) for high-kulīna men reflects the 
systemic polygamy that Sena-era kulīnism institutionalised: kulīna men of 
the highest grade were in such social demand as marriage partners that they 
routinely contracted marriages with dozens of women from lower-grade 
Brahmin families seeking alliance with kulīna prestige. The Panji thus 
documented — and thereby legitimated — a system of social exploitation 
operating through the institution of marriage. 
3.2 Knowledge Production and the Panjikar Institution 
Bourdieu's concept of 'cultural capital' illuminates the Panjikar's social 
position. The Panjikar family — those with hereditary access to the panji 
manuscripts and the professional training to read and interpret them — held a 
monopoly on a form of cultural capital that was irreplaceable in Maithil 
Brahmin society. No marriage among proper Maithil Brahmins could be 
contracted without Panjikar verification. This gatekeeping function translated 
directly into economic capital (consultation fees), social capital (patronage 
networks across the Tirhut region), and symbolic capital (the prestige of 
being the authoritative arbiter of community identity). 
The current project — digitising the panji manuscripts, rendering them in 
Tirhuta and Devanagari, and publishing them in print and digital formats — 
represents a significant transformation of this knowledge economy. By 
making panji data publicly accessible through Shruti Publication and the 
Videha digital platform, the editors (particularly Gajendra Thakur) are 
democratising a knowledge system that was historically the exclusive 
preserve of Panjikar specialists. This democratisation itself has sociological 
dimensions: it potentially disrupts the Panjikar's professional monopoly 
while simultaneously making the system accessible to the broader Maithil 
diaspora. 
3.2.1 The Panji in the Digital Age 
The Prelim-a.pdf document — with its careful documentation of digital 
imaging, 
Tirhuta 
script 
encoding, 
and 
Devanagari 
transliteration 
methodology — represents the Panji system's adaptation to the conditions of

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 357 
what Manuel Castells calls the 'network society.' The panji, originally a 
regional manuscript tradition, is being converted into a digital archive 
accessible to the global Maithil diaspora. This transition raises profound 
questions about the continued social function of the Panjikar specialist when 
the raw data of the panji becomes digitally searchable. 
Benedict Anderson's analysis of 'print capitalism' and its role in creating 
imagined communities is directly applicable here. The print publication of 
the Genealogical Mapping series — and its parallel digital dissemination 
through Videha — is actively constructing a pan-regional and diaspora 
Maithil identity whose social cohesion is anchored in shared genealogical 
memory. The Panji, originally a local manuscript tradition, is becoming an 
instrument of what might be called 'digital print nationalism' for the Maithil 
community. 
3.3 Gender and the Panji System 
A critical sociological reading of the Panji corpus cannot avoid its 
fundamental androcentric structure. The system is entirely organised around 
patrilineal descent (suta — son, for male line) with women appearing only as 
marriage connectors (the sutā/putra formula for daughters) or as maternal kin 
identifiers (the dau formula tracking a bride's natal lineage). Women are thus 
structurally present in the Panji as nodes in alliance networks but absent as 
independent genealogical subjects. 
This structural invisibility reflects what Adrienne Rich identified as 
'compulsory heterosexuality' institutionalised at the level of kinship 
documentation: women's social existence is recorded only in relation to their 
reproductive and marital function within patrilineal networks. The 'dampati 
lekhaka' (husband-wife author) tradition celebrated in Maithili literary 
history has no counterpart in the Panji system, which is structurally incapable 
of recording women's independent social contributions. 
This critique does not diminish the historical value of the Panji data; it 
contextualises it. The Panji's patrilineal structure is consistent with the 
broader gender ideology of Sanskritic Brahminism, and its analysis 
illuminates precisely why feminist historians of South Asia have emphasised 
the need for supplementary sources — women's oral traditions, Madhubani 
art, vernacular literature — to recover women's historical agency within 
Maithil culture.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 358 
4. Genealogical Methodology and Documentary Analysis 
4.1 The Panji's Formal Grammar 
The Panji employs a highly condensed technical grammar for recording 
genealogical information. The core formula observable throughout the punji-
01-Diwali2014 corpus follows a consistent pattern: 
Formula | Meaning 
[Name] suta / sutau / sutā | Son of [Name] / two sons of / daughter(s) of 
[Village] saṃ [Name] dau | From [Village lineage], [Name]'s [maternal 
uncle's] line 
apar / apara | Another / alternatively (marking variant branches) 
mūla | Origin clan/ancestral group 
pr0 / pra0 | Abbreviation for prathama / 'known as' (nickname marker) 
ka / kau | Genitive particle indicating possession/relation 
nī0 / nī | Nī: from (ablative, village-of-origin marker) 
du dau | Dau reduplicated: confirming double maternal lineage reference 
This compact notation system allows an entire genealogical line — spanning 
birth, village-affiliation, gotra, marriage alliance, and branch variations — to 
be recorded in a single Maithili sentence. The economic density of this 
notation is remarkable: a single page of the punji document records dozens of 
individual genealogical facts that would require pages of discursive prose in 
a Western genealogical record. 
4.1.1 Computational Genealogy: The Panji as Database 
Viewed through the lens of contemporary database theory, the Panji is a 
relational database avant la lettre. Each genealogical entry consists of: (1) a 
primary key (individual name), (2) foreign keys linking to the father's entry, 
mother's natal village, and maternal uncle's lineage; (3) attribute fields for 
village-of-origin, gotra, and variant names (prathama/nickname). The mūla-
dau cross-reference system creates a multi-dimensional relational structure 
that makes the Panji conceptually equivalent to a sophisticated two-table 
relational join. 
The quantitative density of the corpus — 3, genealogical relationship 
markers in 77 pages, averaging 44 per page — demonstrates a level of 
structured information density that is remarkable for a pre-computational 
manuscript tradition. The digitisation project documented in Prelim-a.pdf

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 359 
represents the conversion of this analogue relational database into a digital 
format, preserving both the historical record and the structural logic of the 
original system. 
4.2 Chronological Depth and Verification 
The claim of continuous documentation from 450 AD to 2009 AD — a span 
of fifteen-and-a-half centuries — is the most ambitious genealogical claim in 
the South Asian tradition. For comparative perspective: European nobility 
records rarely exceed five centuries of continuous documentation; Chinese 
clan genealogies (zupu) typically document twenty to forty generations; and 
Jewish yichus records, while ancient, are rarely as systematically structured 
as the Panji. 
The Panji's chronological claim rests on several verification pillars: (1) the 
correlation of named individuals with datable historical events (the 
Halāyudha entry, corroborated by independent Sanskrit scholarship placing 
him ca. 1150–1225; Vallāl Sena's historical reign, approximately 1159–1178 
CE); (2) the internal consistency of generational calculations (approximately 
25–30 years per generation would yield 52–62 generations for 1, years, 
consistent with the depth of documentation); and (3) cross-correlation with 
dated Sanskrit inscriptions, copper plate grants (tāmrapaṭṭa), and other 
epigraphic sources from the Tirhut region. 
4.2.1 The Tegachchiya Problem 
The Panji's own internal acknowledgement of 'tegaChiya' (attenuated/lost) 
branches — where lineages have become untraceable through the formal 
record — reveals an important self-limiting mechanism. The table of 
contents of Prelim-a.pdf includes explicit tegaChiya entries for major mūla 
groups: Nagaṛh Ghasoṭa tegaChiya, Ekahara Rūcholī tegaChiya, Darahara 
Rājanpurā tegaChiya. This institutional recognition of genealogical entropy 
suggests a system sophisticated enough to document its own limitations — a 
mark of epistemological maturity that distinguishes the Panji from mere 
hagiographic genealogy. 
4.3 The Mūla Geography: A Micro-Regional Social Cartography 
The geographic distribution of mūla clusters in the Panji corpus constitutes 
what Doreen Massey would call a 'power-geometry' of space: the social 
significance of belonging to a Sodarpur, Māṇḍara, or Khaual lineage is

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 360 
inseparable from the geographic locations these names reference. The 
Sodarpur mūla refers to a cluster of villages in the Darbhanga-Samastipur 
region; Māṇḍara to the Madara/Mandra area; Khaual to the Khauāl area near 
Darbhanga; Kujaulī to Kujholi village; Baherārhi to villages near Saharsa. 
The Panji thus encodes a micro-regional geography of social distinction: 
where you originate genealogically determines your social identity, marriage 
eligibility, and community obligations. This 'genealogical geography' is 
precisely what allows the system to function across such a wide spatial and 
temporal range — the village-name codes carry sufficient information 
density to locate any individual within the entire Maithil Brahmin social 
space. 
5. Comparative Perspectives: Global Genealogical Traditions 
5.1 The Panji and Comparable World Traditions 
The Panji system invites comparison with other major genealogical 
traditions, both to establish its uniqueness and to situate it within the global 
history of genealogical practice. 
Tradition | Comparison with Panji 
Chinese Zupu (clan genealogies) | Similar patrilineal depth but rarely 
institutionally maintained by professional specialists; typically compiled 
retrospectively every 30–60 years rather than continuously updated 
Jewish Yichus records | Comparable function in regulating marriages and 
certifying religious status (Kohen, Levi lineages), but less structured as a 
cross-referenced database 
European Nobility records (Heraldry) | Similar function in certifying social 
status but confined to political/military elite; the Panji covers an entire 
professional-religious community (Maithil Brahmins) 
Maori Whakapapa | Similar depth of genealogical recitation and social 
function, but primarily oral with written records appearing only post-
colonially 
Mormon (LDS) Genealogy | Comparable scale of documentation but 
primarily retrospective and driven by theological (proxy baptism) rather than 
social-regulatory concerns 
Rajput Vansavali records | Directly comparable South Asian parallel: 
hereditary 
genealogists 
(Charans/Bhats) 
maintaining 
warrior-caste 
genealogies, but with less systematic cross-referencing than the Panji

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 361 
The Panji's distinctive features — continuous professional maintenance 
across fifteen centuries, systematic cross-referencing of patrilineal and 
matrilateral lines, geographic encoding, and its function as a live marriage-
regulatory instrument — make it unique among world genealogical 
traditions. No other system combines depth of time, breadth of community 
coverage, structural sophistication, and continued living use in the way the 
Panji does. 
5.2 The Panji and Mithila's Intellectual Heritage 
The Panji system developed in precisely the same intellectual milieu that 
produced the Navya-Nyāya philosophical school of Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya (c. 
1300 CE) — the rigorous epistemological tradition centred in Mithila that 
remains one of the most sophisticated systems of formal logic in world 
philosophy. This parallel development is not coincidental: both the Panji 
system and the Navya-Nyāya tradition reflect the same underlying Maithil 
intellectual culture's commitment to precise definition, systematic cross-
referencing, and the construction of comprehensive structured knowledge 
systems. 
Gaṅgeśa's Tattvacintāmaṇi — with its rigorous analysis of the conditions of 
valid inference (anumāna), the nature of perceptual knowledge (pratyakṣa), 
and the formal structure of knowledge relations — operates on the same 
cultural substrate that produced the Panji's sophisticated relational 
genealogical grammar. Both systems require professional specialists 
(Naiyāyikas and Pañjīkāras respectively), both involve the management of 
complex structured information, and both serve as instruments of social 
legitimation within the Maithil Brahmin community. The Panji is, in a sense, 
the applied social science of a culture whose theoretical science was Navya-
Nyāya. 
6. The Digitisation Project: Stakes and Methodology 
6.1 The Genealogical Mapping Project (Gajendra Thakur, 
Nagendra Kumar Jha, Vidyanand Jha) 
The Genealogical Mapping project — of which the Prelim-a.pdf constitutes 
the editorial front matter of Part 2 — represents a major archival 
intervention. The project undertakes: (1) digital imaging of manuscript panji 
documents; (2) encoding in Tirhuta script (the traditional script of Mithila,

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 362 
historically used for panji manuscripts before the transition to Kaithi and then 
Devanagari); (3) Devanagari transliteration for broader accessibility; and (4) 
systematic editorial organisation into a village-cluster-based indexing 
system. 
This project is doubly significant: it preserves a manuscript tradition at risk 
of physical deterioration while simultaneously converting it into a format 
accessible to the Maithil diaspora and scholarly community worldwide. The 
Shruti Publication imprint — a Delhi-based house with a strong track record 
in Maithil literature — provides the institutional infrastructure for print 
distribution, while the Videha digital platform (videha.co.in) extends access 
to the global digital audience. 
6.1.1 Methodological Innovations 
The editorial methodology of the Genealogical Mapping project makes 
several significant innovations: the insistence on Tirhuta script encoding 
(preserving connection to the historical manuscript tradition even as the text 
is made digitally accessible), the parallel Kaithi-Devanagari bilingual format 
(visible in the punji document's two-column layout), and the systematic 
indexing of over 150 named village-cluster genealogical lines in the table of 
contents of Part 2 alone. 
The inclusion of Panjikar Vidyanand Jha as a co-author alongside scholars 
Gajendra Thakur and Nagendra Kumar Jha is methodologically important: it 
ensures that the professional genealogical expertise of the hereditary Panjikar 
tradition directly informs the editorial decisions, rather than having scholarly 
editors work from manuscripts without specialist genealogical knowledge. 
6.2 Sociological Stakes of Digitisation 
The digitisation of the Panji raises profound sociological questions about the 
future of the system. If the primary tool of Panjikar professional authority — 
the manuscript panji — becomes publicly accessible in digital form, what 
happens to the social institution of the Panjikar? Three possible trajectories 
present themselves: 
First, democratisation: digital accessibility allows Maithil Brahmins 
worldwide to trace their own genealogical lines without requiring Panjikar 
consultation, potentially empowering individuals while diminishing 
professional gatekeeping. Second, scholarly reconstitution: the Panji data, 
once digitalised and indexed, becomes available for historical, demographic,

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 363 
and sociological research that was previously impossible at scale — 
potentially yielding major insights into migration patterns, marriage 
networks, and social change across fifteen centuries. Third, institutional 
adaptation: the Panjikar tradition may adapt by shifting from manuscript 
consultation to digital database management, maintaining expertise while 
changing its technological medium. 
All three trajectories are likely to occur simultaneously. What is certain is 
that the Genealogical Mapping project, by converting the Panji from a 
restricted manuscript tradition into a publicly accessible digital archive, is 
one of the most significant cultural interventions in Maithil intellectual 
history of the past century. 
7. Conclusions 
The Panji Prabandha of Mithila, as documented in the two texts under 
analysis, presents itself as one of the most extraordinary achievements of 
South Asian social organisation: a genealogical system that has functioned 
continuously for fifteen centuries, regulating marriage, certifying social 
identity, and preserving community memory across the entire Tirhut region 
and its global diaspora. 
Anthropologically, the Panji is a uniquely institutionalised form of social 
memory that has navigated the oral-written interface with unusual 
sophistication, maintaining the professional Panjikar tradition while adapting 
across script systems (Tirhuta, Kaithi, Devanagari) and media (manuscript, 
print, digital). Sociologically, it is a technology of social stratification that 
simultaneously maintains Brahmin hierarchy and provides the documentary 
infrastructure for social mobility claims — a Foucauldian knowledge-power 
apparatus that outlasted its royal founders by nine centuries. Genealogically, 
its formal grammar — the mūla-dau cross-referencing system — constitutes 
a 
pre-computational 
relational 
database 
of 
remarkable 
structural 
sophistication. 
The Genealogical Mapping project of Gajendra Thakur, Nagendra Kumar 
Jha, and Panjikar Vidyanand Jha is, within this context, more than a scholarly 
archival project: it is an act of cultural sovereignty, preserving Mithila's most 
significant institutional knowledge system against physical deterioration 
while making it accessible to the global Maithil community. The Panji's 
transition to digital format marks not the end of a tradition but its most

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 366 
 
Chapter 18 
The Dooshan Pañjī of Mithila: A Comprehensive 
Scholarly Report 
THE DOOSHAN PAÑJĪ OF MITHILA: A COMPREHENSIVE 
SCHOLARLY REPORT 
Reference: Gajendra Thakur's Dooshan Pañjī / "The Black Book" (2023) 
I. PRELIMINARY ORIENTATION: WHAT IS THE PAÑJĪ 
SYSTEM? 
To understand the Dooshan Pañjī, one must first understand the broader Pañjī 
system within which it operates — one of the most extraordinary 
genealogical institutions in world history. 
The Pañjī (Pañjī-Prabandha) is the genealogical record system of the Maithil 
Brahmin, Karna Kayastha, and Kshatriya communities of the Mithila region 
(roughly corresponding to the Tirhut/North Bihar and the Terai belt of 
Nepal). It is not merely a family tree in the Western sense. It is a living, 
institutionally maintained, continuously updated archive of lineage, marriage, 
ritual status, caste transgression, and social history — maintained in 
Mithilaksara (Tirhuta script) on palm-leaf (talpatra) and birch-bark 
manuscripts across centuries. 
The document under examination was created by Gajendra Thakur, founder-
editor of Videha, based on his transcription and transliteration of original 
Pañjī manuscripts — work that began in Chaitra 2000 CE and involved the 
conversion of approximately 11, palm-leaf manuscript images from 
Mithilaksara into Devanagari. 
II. TYPOLOGY OF THE PAÑJĪ: THE SEVEN SPECIES

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 367 
The document opens with a taxonomic statement of immense importance for 
understanding the system's internal logic. It states that the Pañjī is of seven 
types: 
1. Mūla Pañjī (Root/Primary Record)— The foundational genealogical 
register, common to all, which traces every family's origins from a defined 
ancestral stock. Before the reforms instituted under Madhav Singh, all Pañjīs 
were structured uniformly in this mode. 
2. Shākhā Pañjī (Branch Record)— A later subdivision, created when 
Darbhanga ruler Madhav Singh (18th century, around 1760 CE / 
approximately 1800 CE by revised dating) ordered the Pañjīkāras (hereditary 
genealogists) to compile branch-specific registers. This gave rise to the 
Shrotriya sub-caste distinction (see Section VII below). Sub-divisions 
include: 
• 
Upa-Tīp ("sub-note" registers, as in the Sotipura records) 
• 
Nagraam (western-border village) records 
3. Gotra Pañjī (Lineage-Clan Record)— Records the gotra (Vedic 
exogamous clan designation, named after ancient Rishis) and traces its 
ancient primordial origin. This serves the exogamy regulation function: no 
two persons of the same gotra may marry. 
4. 
Patra 
Pañjī 
(Leaf/Generation 
Record)— 
In 
circulation 
for 
approximately 500 years. It introduces the Mūlagrāma (ancestral home 
village) as a permanent identifying tag attached to each individual's record. 
This is the reference most commonly invoked during marriage negotiations. 
5. Dooshan Pañjī (Defect/Taint Record)— The subject of this document. 
Dooshan means "defect," "blemish," or "pollution." This register records 
every transgression, anomaly, or departure from endogamous norm in a 
family's lineage: inter-caste marriages, unions with women of unknown 
lineage, Devadāsī mothers, births from widows, unions with Muslims, births 
after a father's death, and so on. This is the "Black Book" of the system. 
6. Utedhā Pañjī (Sapiṇḍa Release Record)— Records the Sapiṇḍa 
relationships (those related through the offering of funeral rice balls up to six 
generations of paternal ancestors), which determine within which boundary 
marriages are prohibited. It lists six generations of ancestors to establish 
when the kinship bar (sapiṇḍa nivṛtti) ceases and marriage becomes 
permissible.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 368 
7. Asvājanya-Patra Pañjī (Non-natural/Irregular Lineage Record)— A 
catch-all register for irregular, unverifiable, or contested genealogical 
situations. 
A significant sub-note:The document mentions that only the Rasarh-Araria 
Pañjī contains the names of women (strī-gaṇaka nāma) within the 
genealogical record. This is a remarkable sociological observation: across the 
vast system, women are ordinarily recorded only functionally — as mothers, 
daughters, wives — rather than by name. The Rasarh-Araria branch is an 
anomaly in this respect, preserving female nominal identity. 
III. HISTORICAL FOUNDATION OF THE PAÑJĪ SYSTEM 
The document provides an invaluable historiographical account of the 
system's origins: 
Harasimhadeva (1294–post-1324 CE), a ruler of the Karnat dynasty of 
Mithila, was the official founder of the Pañjī-Prabandha. He was born in 
1294 CE and ascended the throne in 1307 CE. He was descended 
fromNanyadeva, who founded the Karnat dynasty in 1019 Śaka 
(approximately 1097 CE), as recorded in the verse Nanda-Indu-Shunya-
Shashi Shaka Varshe. 
Harasimhadeva appointed three inaugural genealogists: 
• 
Gunakar Jha — for the Maithil Brahmins 
• 
Shankardatt — for the Karna Kayasthas 
• 
Vijaydatt — for the Kshatriyas 
The current form of the system was formally inaugurated in1248 Śaka / 1326 
CE. This followed Harasimhadeva's flight to Nepal after his defeat 
byGhiyasuddin Tughlaqin 1324–25 CE. 
This dating places the Pañjī system at approximately700 yearsof continuous 
operation as a formal institution — though the document itself claims the 
total recorded tradition encompassesover 1, yearsof genealogical memory, 
reaching back well before the Karnat-period formalization. When considered 
from that longer perspective, it constitutes what the document correctly 
identifies as "the most extensive record of human lineage in world history" 
— genealogies of populations spread across a large geographic region, 
maintained honestly and continuously under conditions of poverty, foreign 
rule, and social disruption.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 369 
The1760 CE reform under Madhav Singh(Mithilesh Maharaja, a 
permanent settlement zamindar under the British Cornwallis system) ordered 
the Pañjīkāras to compile Shākhā Pustaks (branch-record books). The 
document makes an important claim here: the Shrotriya sub-caste as a 
distinct social categorydid not exist before approximately 1800 CEin the 
British India part of Mithila. Before this, "Shrotriya" simply referred to one 
engaged in Vedic study — an educational designation, not a caste sub-
identity. This distinction between the Indian and Nepali portions of Mithila is 
crucial: in the Nepal portion, Shrotriya retains its original meaning 
(educational stream), while in British India it became a hereditary sub-caste 
designation, partly through the upgradation-for-cash system of which the 
book provides documentary evidence in its annexures. 
IV. THE DOOSHAN PAÑJĪ: ITS NATURE, PURPOSE, AND 
SOCIAL LOGIC 
A. What Is "Dooshan"? 
Dooshan (Sanskrit: dūṣaṇa) literally means "that which contaminates, 
pollutes, or taints." In the genealogical context, it refers to any marriage 
alliance or birth circumstance that introduces an element of jāti-bhedī (caste-
crossing), anapekṣita (unverifiable lineage), or dharma-bahirāgata (normative 
transgression) into a Brahmin family line. 
The Dooshan Pañjī is, in the words of the document itself, "the record of the 
erosion (kṣaraṇa) that has occurred in a lineage." This is an important 
framing: kṣaraṇa means a gradual wearing away or leakage, suggesting the 
system's metaphor is one of genealogical purity as an integrity to be 
maintained over time, with recorded "breaches." 
B. Why Record Transgressions at All? 
This is perhaps the most intellectually striking feature of the Dooshan Pañjī. 
The obvious function of a genealogical system in a caste society is to enforce 
endogamy — to prevent inter-caste marriage. Why, then, record what one 
wishes to prevent? 
The document offers a sophisticated answer: the recording happenslong after 
the event(bahut bādame ekara charca sambhava hoita achi), precisely when 
the tainted lineage has been "laundered" through successive generations of 
endogamous marriage and when its descendants are now fully integrated into

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 370 
Brahmin society. The example ofDevadhar(Table 1) is paradigmatic: 
Gunawati Himsha, a woman of "another caste," married Chamaru of 
Valiyasa lineage; their union produced Gangadhar. Through the nextsix 
generationsof daughters and sons-in-law drawn entirely from recognized 
Brahmin Mūlas (Sarisav, Sakaradhi, Panichobh, Darihara, Pali, Narwal), the 
family was considered purnatah shuddha — completely purified. Only at that 
point is the historical record, previously suppressed, published in the 
Dooshan Pañjī. 
The sociological function is thereforeforward-looking, not punitive.The 
document states explicitly: the purpose is that related lineages might avoid 
the dupariṇāma (bad consequence) of unknowingly marrying back into a 
lineage whose taint has not yet completed the six-generation purification 
cycle. It is, in modern terms, a form of genealogical epidemiology — a 
tracking system for recessive social "risk factors" in marriage networks. 
C. The Paradox of Social Liberalism and the Dooshan Pañjī 
The document makes a culturally significant and counterintuitive argument: 
the existence of the Dooshan Pañjī is evidence not of Mithila's casteism but 
of its relativesocial openness and reformability. The argument runs as 
follows: 
In a society of total, rigid, unforgiving caste orthodoxy, inter-caste unions 
would either be completely suppressed (and thus invisible to any 
documentary record) or would result in permanent, hereditary ostracism with 
no mechanism for reintegration. The Dooshan Pañjī demonstrates that 
Mithila society had: 
• 
A formal mechanism for eventual reintegration — the six-generation 
purification protocol 
• 
An honest documentary impulse — a willingness to record rather than 
erase 
• 
No permanent hatred (vidveṣa) as a lasting social disposition — the 
document states that even "burning questions" (dalmalaita karaewāli 
ghaṭanā) become kālātīta (time-transcendent, obsolete) and are forgotten 
The document frames Mithila society as udāra (liberal) and namanaśīla 
(flexible) in comparison with other Indian civilizations of the same period, 
noting the absence of dhārmika kaṭṭarapana (religious fundamentalism) as a 
permanent feature of the Maithil social character.

V. SOCIAL CONTENT OF THE DOOSHAN PAÑJĪ: THE 97 
TABLES 
The heart of the document consists of 97 genealogical tables, each recording 
a specific "tainted" marriage event or lineage anomaly, followed by the 
subsequent generational chain. These tables are not random — they 
constitute a structured typological survey of every kind of normative 
transgression recorded in the broader Pañjī corpus. 
The following categories of "dooshan" (transgression type) emerge from 
analysis of all 97 tables: 
A. Inter-Caste Marriage with Specific Named Communities 
1. Charmakārī/Charmakāriṇī (Cobbler/Tanner women):This is the most 
extensively documented category in the Dooshan Pañjī, and it carries world-
historical significance because it includes the genealogy ofGaṅgeśa 
Upādhyāya, the founder of the Navya-Nyāya (New Logic) school of Indian 
philosophy (see Section VI below). 
Table 24 (Entry 211/1): Records "Ānandā Charmakārī" (ānandā 211/1) — a 
Cobbler-community woman — whose lineage extends through Talhanapur, 
Takwal, Darihara, Panchobh, Merandi, Brahmapur, connected to the lineage 
of "Baiju." 
Tables 49–51 (Entries 188/2, 189/1, 189/II): These are the Gaṅgeśa entries 
— recording that the wife (Vallabhā) of Gaṅgeśa, author of the 
Tattvachintāmaṇi, was a Charmakāriṇī (a woman of the tanner/cobbler 
community), and that Gaṅgeśa was born pitṛ-parokṣe pañca-varṣa-vyatīte — 
five years after the death of his father. 
Tables 50, 51 (189/1, 189/II): Another lineage involving Charmakāriṇī 
Medhā — her descendants extend through Sodarpur, Alay, Mandar, Naraun, 
Panichobh, Khandvala, Deeh Darihara, Brahmapur, and Korai. 
Tables 63, 78 (195/II, 203/II): Record Hiruā Charmakār (a male cobbler-
caste individual) whose daughter married a Brahmin of Pali Mūla, producing 
a lineage extending through Budwal, Sakradhi, Mandar, Fanandah, Pakliya, 
Alay, Belmohon. 
2. Rājaka (Washerman community) women:Tables 29–30 (207/1, 207/II): 
Record marriages in Rajak (rājaka dharma / rājaka mānakī) tradition, with 
lineages extending through Khandvala, Budwal, Mandar, Takwal, Valiyasa, 
Brahmapur, and Meranda. A particularly notable detail: Table 30 records

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 372 
Rājaka Paramparā (washerman tradition) with the note "rājaka parivārā 
mātrikā" — the maternal lineage is explicitly from a washerman family. 
Tables 54–55 (191/I, 191/II), 64–65 (196/I, 196/II): Record Haḍiṇī 
Rudramatī Rājaka paramparā — a woman named Rudramati from the 
washerman (Rājaka) tradition. The Brahmin lineage from Khauwal 
(Sadhukar–Shrikar–Vibhakar) married into this line. The records track 
subsequent generations through Mandar, Valihara, Didhoi. 
3. Caṇḍāla / Chamar (Untouchable castes) — general:Several entries 
involve unions with persons described simply as niṣiddha-kula ("prohibited 
lineage") or classified in the Gadahā Paṇḍoli village category, which appears 
as a terminal designation for progeny of such unions in multiple tables (35–
43, spanning 181/1 through 183/2). The repeated appearance of "Arvind — 
Nidhi — niṣiddha-kula" in Gadahā Paṇḍoli across many entries suggests this 
village served as a settlement zone for such socially ambiguous lineages. 
4. Gopa / Ahir (Cowherd caste) women:Table 52 (190/1, Haḍiṇī 
Rudramatī): Records Jagnath–Rudramati marriage as goṅga (ceremonially 
marked, irregular) — a specific vocabulary for inter-caste unions. 
Table 56 (192/I): Lagārī Gopāī — "Gopāī of the Lagari (shepherd/cowherd) 
community." Records Gopaputrī (cowherd's daughter) marriage in Sodarpur-
Karmahe lineage. 
5. Taṃtī / Tāntī (Weaver community) women:Table 48 (188/1): 
Shrinathasuta — a daughter of Shrinath who married into the Tāṃtī jāti 
(weaver community). The table records the lineage through Tantani, 
Sodarpur, Sakradhi, Jalwal, Khandvala, Bharathi. 
6. Tailika (Oil-presser community):Table 61 (194/II): Tailika Devāī — an 
oil-presser woman named Devai. The lineage involves Khandvala, Tisounta, 
Pandol, Khajuri, Sakradhi, with the note tailika Devāī antaḥjātīya (inter-caste 
with Tailika Devai). 
7. Dhīvara (Fisherman community) women:Table 96 (221/1): Dhīvara 
Dāsī — "Fisherman-community servant woman." Mandan's wife is noted as 
belonging to this category. The lineage extends through Kujjauli, Mandar, 
Naraun, Suragan, Nikhuti. 
8. Kocha / Assam Royal lineage:Table 9 (208/1): Kocha-rāja patnī Sohāgo 
— "Sohago, wife of a Koch (Assamese) raja." The Koch (Kochrajvanshi) 
were a northeast India royal dynasty. This entry records a Brahmin lineage 
(Khandvala, Bhauwal, Bhauwal, Mandar, Takwal, Valiyasa, Brahmapur,

Gajendra Thakur — 373 
Merandi, Korthwa) with a connection to the Koch royal house. Entry says 
explicitly: "Puttrikā nāma Sohāgo" — daughter named Sohago from the 
Assam Koch royal lineage. 
B. Muslim/Yavana Lineage Entries 
1. Yavana Daulatijahān:Tables 11–12 (196/7, 216/1): Dagara kī māta 
(Dagara's mother) is Yavana Daulatijahāṃ — a Muslim woman named 
Daulatijahan. Her lineage is carefully tracked through Pali–Satlakha–
Gangoli–Nadam. The Brahmin lineage (from Pavauli, Ekahra, Jagati, 
Sodarpur, Pali, Satlakha, Gangoli) has a Muslim maternal ancestor. 
Table 20 (213/1): Jānika (place), Babhniyal Mūla — the entry records 
Shrīrāmaka-māta (Devadāsī) in the Valiyasa column, meaning Ram's mother 
was a Devadāsī, and also involves a Yavana connection. 
2. Pashchim Desha āgata (Arrivals from the West — unidentified 
lineage):Tables 22–23 (212/1, 212/2): These are genealogically enigmatic 
entries: persons who came from Pashchim Desha (western regions — 
possibly referring to Awadh, Rajputana, or even further west) with ajñāta 
kula-śīla — unknown or unverifiable lineage and character. The table records 
Mandar-Horai-Madhusudana-suta (entry: "Madhusudana's daughter of 
unknown lineage") in one case, and in the second case a person from 
Pashchim Desha whose lineage is tracked through Mandar, Khandvala, 
Darihara, Vaheradhi, Khauwal, and Jalaya. 
3. Chāṃpo Yavana (Muslim woman named Champo):Table 66 (197/I): 
Chāmpo Yavana — a Muslim woman named Champo. The Brahmin lineage 
(Mandar, Pachahi, Pavauli, Pakliya, Tirlathi, Karnaitha) has a Muslim 
maternal ancestor, specifically as the wife of a person of the Koiyara Mūla. 
C. Devadāsī Lineage Entries 
The Devadāsī (temple-dancer/courtesan) entries are among the most 
sociologically significant in the document, as they reveal a formally 
documented interface between the Brahmin genealogical system and the 
temple-service community. Tables 16–21 (215/1 through 213/2) form a 
coherent cluster all labeled Jānika Devadāsa (servant of the divine / 
Devadasa) — a specific category within the Pañjī.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 374 
Table 16 (215/1):Records Harihara kī māta (Devadāsī) — Harihara's mother 
was a Devadasi. Connected to Sodarpur, Khauwal, Sodarpur, Darihara, 
Panichobh, Chando, Tilay, and Khauwal. 
Table 17 (215/2):Records Vidyāpati kī māta (Devadāsī) — Vidyāpati's 
mother was a Devadasi. Connected to Budwal, Khauwal, Jaliwal, Mandar, 
Kurhan, Tilay. 
Table 18 (214/1):Records Lākhū kī māta (Devadāsī) — Lakhu's mother was 
a Devadasi. From Sodarpur, Pali, Suragan, Darihara, Tilay-Shripati, 
Khauwal, Sodarpur. 
Table 19 (214/2):Records Jāge kī māta (Devadāsa) — Jage's mother was a 
Devadasi. From Sodarpur, Mandar, Hariam, Mandar, Karmahe, Khauwal, 
Sodarpur. 
Table 20 (213/1):Jānika Devadāsa — Records Shrīrāmaka māta (Devadāsī) 
in Valiyasa. The father's name for this entry's child is listed as Babhniyal. 
Table 21 (213/2):Jānika Devadāsa — Records Vidyāpati kī māta (Devadāsī) 
via Tilay-Shripati–Khauwal route. 
There is also a fascinating sub-cluster in Table 16: the Tilaya Shripati 
column across multiple Devadasi entries consistently links to a woman 
identified only as Vidyāpati kī māta (Devadāsī) — raising the possibility that 
this refers to the mother of the legendary poet Vidyāpati (ca. 1352–1448 CE), 
though the document does not make this claim explicitly. 
The broader point: the Devadasi entries document a systematic, recurring 
interface between Brahmin scholarly families and the temple-arts community 
of Mithila, particularly in the Tilay, Khauwal, Sodarpur, and Jaliwal areas. 
This is not the record of isolated lapses but of an institutional pattern. 
D. Widow Remarriage / Vidhavā Union Entries 
Table 28:Vidhavā (widow) — the lineage of a son born of a widow is 
tracked through Sodarpur–Mandar–Gaul–Valiyasa–Monari–Pali–Kurisama–
Kewtaram Pandoli. The mother is not named; she is identified only by 
marital status. 
Table 94 (220/1):Vārtinī Vidhavā (a wandering widow / vārtinī suggests a 
woman who engages in occupational work, i.e., economically active) — 
records the descendants of her son Ganpati-Chand from Pali, Pavauli, 
Mandar, Purisama, Pandauli lineages.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 375 
E. Guru-Patnī (Teacher's Wife) Union 
Table 82 (205/1):Guru-patnī — Records Bhaṭṭabhūṣaṇa of the Marrar Mūla 
who "married a guru's wife" (guru-patnī se vivāha kala). This represents one 
of the gravest categories of moral transgression in the dharmaśāstra 
framework (violation of the teacher-disciple relationship's sexual taboo). Yet 
here it is documented, with the lineage tracked through Khandvala, Karmahe, 
Naraun, Terhota, Kujjauli, without permanent erasure. The lineage is simply 
noted, and descendants are tracked. 
F. Patha Patitā (Fallen Woman / Woman of Ill Repute) Entries 
Tables 85, 93 (206/2, 219/2):Patha Patitā — literally "fallen on the road," a 
woman of morally compromised status (prostitute or equivalent). Two 
separate entries record Lakshesvara Hore who is described as having married 
a Gangoli path patitā kanyā (a fallen woman from Gangoli). One of these 
entries (206/2) is cross-referenced with a Parambrāhmaṇa (highest Brahmin) 
lineage connection, creating a particularly stark juxtaposition. 
G. Parambrāhmaṇa (Mahāpātra) / Higher Brahmin 
Transgressions 
Tables 79–81, 84 (203/1, 204/1, 204/II, 206/1):The Parambrāhmaṇa 
(Mahāpātra) entries record that Lakshesvara Hore, of a "highest Brahmin" 
status, married into various compromised lineages including the Gangoli 
fallen woman. The Parambrāhmaṇa designation in this context appears to be 
a title given to a lineage, perhaps associated with the Mahāpātra community 
of ritual specialists whose function involved contact with death pollution. 
H. Antaḥjātīya (Inter-caste, General) 
The term antaḥjātīya or antarjātīya (literally "between-caste" — i.e., inter-
caste) appears as a general-purpose notation in multiple tables (Tables 41, 45, 
47, 54, 60, 61, 64, 65) where the specific caste of the non-Brahmin partner is 
not named but the inter-caste nature of the union is recorded. This suggests 
the Pañjīkāras did not always have detailed information about the partner's 
caste identity, but recorded the fact of transgression nonetheless. 
I. Ajñāta (Unknown Lineage) Entries 
A recurring formula across dozens of tables is ajñāta — "unknown" or 
"unknowable." This appears most often for maternal lineages: "Rāmaka

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 376 
mātṛka ajñāta" (Ram's maternal lineage unknown), "Harikarakī māta ajñāta" 
(Harikar's mother unknown), and so on. The ajñāta designation is itself a 
category of "dooshan" — uncertainty about maternal origin is treated as a 
form of genealogical taint, since the Pañjī system requires bilateral 
verification of lineage for a "clean" marriage alliance. 
This is anthropologically significant: it means that the Dooshan Pañjī 
preserves evidence of alarge class of womenin Mithila society — from 
Devadāsīs to unmarried mothers to women from communities that kept no 
genealogical records — who could not be fully documented within the 
Brahmin genealogical framework. Their descendants are tracked but their 
own identities are rendered as ajñāta, a sociological ghost. 
VI. THE GANGESA UPADHYAYA ENTRIES: A CASE STUDY 
OF GENEALOGICAL SUPPRESSION AND SCHOLARLY 
INJUSTICE 
The most explosive content of the Dooshan Pañjī concernsGaṅgeśa 
Upādhyāya(approximately 13th–14th century CE), the founder of Navya-
Nyāya — the New Logic school of Indian philosophy, headquartered at 
Mithila, which produced the most technically sophisticated epistemological 
system in the history of Indian thought. His magnum opus, the 
Tattvachintāmaṇi ("Jewel of Reflection on the Truth"), is the foundational 
text of the school. 
A. What the Pañjī Records 
Table 49 (188/2):The entry reads: Charmakāriṇī (woman of the 
cobbler/tanner community) → Chhadan Mūla → Gaṅgeśa, author of 
Tattvachintāmaṇi. His wife (Vallabhā) was from the Chhadan Mūla. The 
entry in the Dooshan Pañjī records her origin as Charmakāriṇī — a Dalit 
woman of the leather-working community. 
Entry 21//10 (Mūla Pañjī):"Chhādan-sañ Jagadguru Gaṅgeś — author of 
Tattvachintāmaṇi. From the Chhadan lineage, the wife (Vallabhā) of 
Gaṅgeśa, author of Tattvachintāmaṇi, was a Charmakāriṇī. It is recorded that 
Gaṅgeśa was born pitṛ-parokṣe pañca-varṣa vyatīte — five years after the 
passing of his father." 
The expression pitṛ-parokṣe pañca-varṣa vyatīte Gaṅgeśotpattiḥ ("Gaṅgeśa's 
birth came five years after [his] father's absence/death") is noted as an

B. What Was Suppressed and by Whom 
The document launches a detailed scholarly critique of two figures: 
Prof. Ramanath Jha(20th century Maithil Sanskrit scholar): Despite being a 
leading authority on the Pañjī system, Ramanath Jha provided information 
about Gaṅgeśa toDinesh Chandra Bhattacharyafor the latter's History of 
Navya-Nyāya in Mithila (1958) that was deliberately incomplete. 
Bhattacharya, working from Ramanath Jha's inputs, wrote that "Gangesa's 
family is completely ignored and we are not expected to know even his 
father's name," that "the family which was inferior in social status is now 
extinct in Mithila," and that all descendants of the family "dwindled into 
insignificance again and became extinct soon after his son's death." The 
document characterizes this as a total falsehood — made possible only 
because Ramanath Jha withheld the Dooshan Pañjī evidence, which was 
known to him. 
Udayanath Jha 'Ashok'(recipient of Sahitya Akademi's Bhasha Samman): 
His Sahitya Akademi monograph on Gaṅgeśa (2016) similarly failed to 
engage with the genealogical record despite its availability since 2009. The 
document characterizes this as a continuation of the same casteist agenda 
under a new institutional banner. 
The charge is specific and serious: both scholars were aware (or should have 
been aware, post-2009) that Gaṅgeśa married a woman of the Charmakāra

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 378 
(Dalit cobbler) community, and that this fact was recorded in the Pañjī. They 
suppressed or avoided this fact, which the author terms an"honour 
killing"— the posthumous social erasure of Gaṅgeśa's identity and lineage in 
order to protect the narrative of Navya-Nyāya as the pure product of high-
caste Brahmin intellectual culture. 
C. The Wider Philosophical-Historical Stakes 
The document sets the Gaṅgeśa entries in a wider context of significance: 
• 
Gaṅgeśa held the title Jagadguru AND Paramguru — the highest 
possible academic honorific in medieval India, equivalent in function (as 
the document notes) to "Distinguished University Professor." The 
document notes that only Nutan Vāchaspati later shared this dual honor. 
• 
The transmission of Navya-Nyāya to Bengal: Vasudev of Bengal came to 
Mithila for study, memorized the Tattvachintāmaṇi, and returned to 
Bengal. Pakshadhar Mishra and other Mithila scholars refused to allow 
the text to be copied — a deliberate act of intellectual protectionism. 
Vasudev's disciple Raghunath Shiromani eventually established Navya-
Nyāya in Navadvipa (Bengal) after defeating his guru Pakshadhar in a 
shāstrārtha (scriptural debate). This transfer of Navya-Nyāya out of 
Mithila into Bengal, where it flourished independently, is framed in the 
document as the "revenge of nature" against the honour-killing of 
Gaṅgeśa's family — the school lost its homeland because Mithila's 
scholarly establishment prioritized caste purity over intellectual 
inheritance. 
• 
Gangesa's Descendant Chain (Authenticated): The document provides 
multiple cross-references from the Devānand Pañjī proving that the 
family did not go extinct as Bhattacharya/Ramanath Jha claimed. Supan 
(son), Harāditya (grandson through daughter), Bhaveśvara at Sandagahi, 
Varddhamāna (direct son), Varddhamāna's daughter's children — these 
are all documented. 
• 
The literary reflex: Gaṅgeśa's son Varddhamāna addressed his father as 
sukavikairavakananenduh — "the moon to the white-lotus garden of 
good poets" — a phrase that suggests Gaṅgeśa was also a poet of 
distinction, though the conspiracy of social erasure has resulted in none 
of his poetry surviving in accessible form. 
• 
International scholarship: The document cites Stephen H. Phillips 
(University of Texas), who completed the first full English translation of
