records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 379 
all four sections of the Tattvachintāmaṇi (Bloomsbury Academic, 2020), 
and V.P. Bhatta, who completed another full four-volume translation by 
2025 (Eastern Book Linkers, Delhi). Both works validate the 
international significance of Gaṅgeśa's contribution — but neither was 
possible to fully contextualize without the genealogical background that 
was suppressed for over a century. The document quotes Phillips's 
Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy entry, noting that genealogical 
records suggest Gaṅgeśa "had a wife and three sons and a daughter. One 
child was the famous Nyaya author, Vardhamana." 
D. TheShabdashāstramConnection 
The author (Gajendra Thakur) wrote a Maithili short story Shabdashāstram 
("The Science of Words") based on the Pañjī records of Gaṅgeśa, specifically 
on the relationship with Ānandā Charmakāriṇī. The story was translated into 
English as "The Science of Words" and published in Indian Literature 
(Sahitya Akademi journal), Vol. 58, No. 2 (March/April 2014), pp. 78–93. 
This is a remarkable case of genealogical data generating literary fiction that 
was then published in the very journal of the institution accused of 
suppressing the underlying historical truth. 
VII. THE SHROTRIYA QUESTION: CASTE SUB-IDENTITY 
AND COLONIAL GENEALOGY 
The document contains an important socio-historical argument about 
theShrotriya sub-castewhose implications reach beyond Mithila: 
Before approximately 1800 CE (after Madhav Singh's reforms, which were 
formalized under British permanent settlement), the term Shrotriya in Maithil 
Brahmin contexts meant someone engaged in Vedic education — an 
educational-occupational designation. There was no Shrotriya as an inherited 
caste sub-identity. 
The innovation of Shrotriya as a heritable sub-caste was: 
• 
Triggered by Madhav Singh's 1760 CE order for Shākhā Pañjī 
compilation 
• 
Formalized under British permanent settlement (Cornwallis era) 
• 
Operationalized through upgradation-for-cash — the document mentions 
that the Pañjī book's annexure contains copies of "genealogy-based 
upgradation orders (proof of upgradation for cash)," meaning that

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 380 
families could purchase their elevation to Shrotriya sub-caste status 
through the genealogical bureaucracy 
This means the Shrotriya/non-Shrotriya distinction within Maithil 
Brahminism is acolonial-era construction, not an ancient social reality. In 
Nepal's portion of Mithila, where the British permanent settlement system 
did not apply, "Shrotriya" retains its educational meaning to this day. 
This finding has profound implications for understanding how caste identities 
in general were hardened, formalized, and made heritable through the 
intersection of indigenous genealogical systems and colonial administrative-
legal frameworks. 
VIII. WOMEN IN THE DOOSHAN PAÑJĪ: A FEMINIST 
ANTHROPOLOGICAL READING 
The Dooshan Pañjī, despite being a record of male lineages, provides — 
precisely through its catalog of "defects" — one of the richest archives of 
female social experience in pre-modern Mithila. 
The Categories of Documented Women 
Devadāsī Women:At least seven entries (Tables 16–21 and cross-referenced 
in 
the 
summary 
tables) 
document 
women 
who 
were 
temple 
performers/courtesan-class artists. Their sons are named and tracked; their 
own names are occasionally preserved (Vidyāpati kī māta, Harikara kī māta), 
though often they are identified only by function. The document notes that 
women like Devadāsī Santati in multiple entries were the mothers of children 
"now honored in society." 
Widow Mothers (Vidhavā):Tables 28 and 94 specifically mark lineages of 
widows' sons — a significant fact because widow remarriage (or widow 
sexual activity leading to birth) was a major social transgression in upper-
caste north Indian society of the medieval period. The Pañjī records these not 
for condemnation but for genealogical accuracy. 
Wandering/Working Widows (Vārtinī Vidhavā):The vārtinī designation 
suggests a widow who engaged in vārtā (economic activity, trade) for 
livelihood. This is a woman who, by definition, moved in public economic 
space — unusual for upper-caste women. 
Women of Unknown Identity (Ajñāta Māta):Dozens of entries record 
children whose mothers are simply ajñāta — unknown. These women left no

Gajendra Thakur — 381 
documentary trace except the children they bore. The system required 
bilateral documentation for marriage purity, so any child whose maternal 
lineage could not be verified was automatically placed in the Dooshan 
register. 
The Rājaka Rudramatī:Rudramatī (from the washerman community) 
appears in multiple entries (Tables 52–55, 64–65) as a named individual 
whose descendants are extensively tracked. She is one of the few non-
Brahmin women in the document who is consistently identified by name 
rather than only by community designation. 
The Charmakāriṇī Medhā:Another named Dalit woman (Table 50, 189/1) 
whose lineage extends across ten genealogical stops — a breadth of 
documented impact that rivals many Brahmin women in the mainstream 
Pañjī. 
The Rasarh-Araria Anomaly:As noted, only this regional sub-tradition of 
the Pañjī records women's names systematically. The contrast underscores 
how profoundly women were rendered genealogically invisible in the main 
system — present as biological conduits but absent as named social agents. 
What This Tells Us 
The Dooshan Pañjī, inadvertently, preserves a counter-archive of female 
social diversity in Mithila: cobblers, tanners, washerwomen, temple 
performers, wandering widows, Muslim women (Daulatijahāṃ, Chāmpo), 
and the unnamed mothers of an entire Brahmin genealogy. None of these 
women appear in the canonical literary or historical records of Mithila. They 
exist only because the genealogical system needed to explain anomalies in 
male Brahmin lineages. 
IX. THE NADAM–YAVANA CONNECTION AND INTER-
RELIGIOUS GENEALOGY 
The Yavana (Muslim/foreigner) entries in the Dooshan Pañjī deserve 
particular attention as a window into the social history of Hindu-Muslim 
coexistence at the village and family level in medieval Mithila. 
Daulatijahāṃappears twice (Tables 11–12, 196/7 and 216/1) as the maternal 
ancestor of Brahmin lineages. She is called Yavana Daulatijahāṃ — 
Daulatijahan the Muslim woman — and identified as Dagara kī māta (mother 
of Dagara), connected to Nadam village in one entry and the Gangoli–

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 382 
Khauwal region in another. This means that at some point (the generation 
before the entry's earliest recorded ancestor), a Brahmin man had a child with 
a Muslim woman named Daulatijahan. That child's descendants married back 
into Brahmin society and, by the time the Dooshan Pañjī records them, are 
themselves Brahmins — though the maternal Muslim origin is documented. 
Chāmpo Yavana(Table 66, 197/1) is the patnī (wife) of a Koiyara lineage 
man from the Mandar-Pachahi-Pavauli region. Her children are tracked into 
Brahmin society. 
Pashchim Desha āgataentries (Tables 22–23, 212/1, 212/2): Two separate 
families who "came from the west" with unknown lineage (ajñāta kula-śīla) 
are documented. The west may refer to Mughal-administered territories or to 
communities outside the Maithil genealogical network entirely. 
The document contextualizes these entries within its broader argument about 
Mithila's social liberalism: the entries document what happened, they do not 
prescribe condemnation. The fact that descendants of Daulatijahan are now 
part of Brahmin society — without the Muslim origin erasing their Brahmin 
status after six generations — mirrors exactly the purification logic that 
applies to all other "tainted" lineages. 
X. KARTOGRAPHY OF THE DOOSHAN PAÑJĪ: THE MŪLA-
GRĀMA NETWORK 
The 97 tables together function as an involuntary social geography of 
Mithila, mapping the genealogical network of approximately 50–60 named 
Mūla (ancestral home villages) across the region. These include: 
Core Maithil Brahmin Mūla villagesthat appear as the primary nodes of the 
genealogical network: Sodarpur, Sarlakha, Khandvala, Darihara, Khauwal, 
Mandar, Pali, Budwal (Budhwal), Valiyasa, Hariam, Karmahe, Sakradhi, 
Panauli (Pavauli), Ghusaut, Panichobh, Naraun, Sarisav, Ekahra, Jagati, 
Alay, Takhwal, Fakandah (Fanandah), Simsim, Tirladi, Kujjauli, Sargana, 
Pachahi. 
Peripheral or "defect-associated" terminal nodes: Gadahā Paṇḍoli, 
Belmohana, Korai, Nadam, Sinhasrama, Jalayal, Gorhini, Suiri, Pakliya, 
Simbhunam, Bhringi — these appear repeatedly as the endpoints of lineages 
that have absorbed the "tainted" element and are tracked until purification or 
integration is documented.

This geography is not static. The document records migrations (pravaas-
āpravaas) — people who moved from their ancestral villages and whose new 
locations are tracked. The Pañjī thus also functions as a migration record, 
documenting how Mithila's Brahmin population dispersed and reconcentrated 
across the region over centuries. 
XI. CRITICAL ASSESSMENT: THE PANJI AS WORLD 
HERITAGE DOCUMENT 
Uniqueness in World Genealogical History 
The document makes the claim — and it is well-supported — thatno other 
known society in world historyhas produced a genealogical record of 
comparable scope, duration, and geographic spread maintained under 
comparable conditions of adversity. The key parameters: 
• 
Duration: 700 years of formal institutional record (1326 CE to present), 
with genealogical memory reaching back to approximately 450 CE in the 
oldest strata 
• 
Geographic scope: Hundreds of villages across Bihar and Nepal 
• 
Population coverage: Every Maithil Brahmin, Karna Kayastha, and 
Kshatriya family in the region 
• 
Conditions: Maintained under Muslim sultanate rule (Tughlaq, later 
Mughal), British colonial rule, extreme agrarian poverty, social 
disruption, and without institutional state support for centuries 
• 
Intellectual integrity: The honest recording of transgressive material (the 
Dooshan Pañjī itself being the clearest example) rather than a sanitized 
genealogy 
The 2009 Digitization and Release 
Gajendra Thakur began transcription work in Chaitra 2000 CE, converting 
Mithilaksara manuscripts to Devanagari. In 2009, the Dooshan Pañjī was 
released publicly — both in book form (Pañjī Prabandha, parts I and II, 
available at videha.co.in/pothi.htm) and as DVDs containing 11, palm-leaf 
image JPEGs. The release caused intense controversy: the document 
mentions verbal abuse (in person and by phone), ongoing resentment, and the 
phenomenon of people becoming angry when the Dooshan Pañjī's contents 
are mentioned — described vividly as "jeno khūna pībi jeta" ("as if they 
would drink blood").

ia601402.us.archive.org/31/items/maithili_202209/Gajendra_Thakur_Doosh

The manuscripts were also uploaded to Google Books in 2009 and to the 
Videha website.Anshuman Pandey(University of Michigan, doctoral 
dissertation 2014: Recasting the Brahmin in Medieval Mithila: Origins of 
Caste Identity among the Maithil Brahmins of North Bihar) acknowledges 
receiving a complete digitized set of Pañjī records from Gajendra Thakur in 
2007, noting that Pañjīkāras (hereditary genealogists) are usually reluctant to 
allow outside access to these records. 
XII. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORKS FOR 
UNDERSTANDING THE DOOSHAN PAÑJĪ 
Anthropological 
The Dooshan Pañjī represents what Victor Turner would recognize as a 
record of liminality — the tracking of individuals and lineages in a threshold 
state, between categories, neither fully inside nor outside the social structure. 
The six-generation purification protocol is a formal rite of passage for 
lineages, not individuals, operating across multiple human lifetimes. 
Claude Lévi-Strauss's analysis of kinship systems would recognize the Pañjī 
system as a unique empirical database for the study of alliance theory: it 
documents not just who married whom, but every case where the marriage 
rules were broken, and the subsequent genealogical management of those 
breaches. 
Sociological 
In terms of Weberian sociology, the Pañjī represents a form ofrationalized 
status management— a bureaucratic system for the social construction and 
maintenance of honor (Ehre) as a collective property of lineages. The 
Dooshan Pañjī is the "negative ledger" in this honor-accounting system. 
From a Durkheimian perspective, the system's persistence through centuries 
of political disruption demonstrates the extraordinary power ofcollective 
representation— the Pañjī as a shared social fact that transcends individual 
memory and reproduces the community's self-understanding across time. 
Genealogical/Historical

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 385 
The document's argument about Shrotriya as a colonial-era construction 
resonates strongly withNicholas Dirks'sthesis in Castes of Mind (2001): that 
caste as a rigid, heritable, administratively manageable category was partly a 
product of British colonial enumeration and governance rather than an 
unchanged ancient institution. The Pañjī evidence here is particularly 
valuable because it is indigenous, pre-colonial documentation that shows 
caste categories in process, permeable, and subject to reformulation. 
XIII. CONCLUSION: THE SIGNIFICANCE OF THE 
DOOSHAN PAÑJĪ 
The Dooshan Pañjī of Mithila is simultaneously: 
A genealogical documentof extraordinary scope and duration — the most 
ambitious sustained project of human lineage-recording in recorded history. 
A social historyof Mithila across 700+ years, documenting the lived reality 
of caste transgression, inter-community contact, social mobility, female 
agency, and community resilience that official histories have systematically 
ignored. 
A philosophical archive— its most explosive entry concerns the founder of 
India's most technically sophisticated epistemological tradition, and reveals 
that Navya-Nyāya was born not in the rarefied atmosphere of high-caste 
Brahmin exclusivity but from a man whose wife was a Dalit cobbler-woman, 
born five years after his father's death — a biography that only the Dooshan 
Pañjī preserves. 
A political document— its 2009 release triggered immediate controversy 
because it challenges the caste hierarchies and purity narratives that continue 
to organize Maithil Brahmin social life. The suppression of the Gaṅgeśa 
entries by Ramanath Jha and Udayanath Jha is not merely an intellectual 
failure but an act of what the author correctly names "honour killing" — the 
posthumous erasure of an identity because it does not conform to the social 
hierarchy. 
A document of social healing— in its own terms, the Dooshan Pañjī is not 
an instrument of shaming but of liberation. Its logic is that recorded 
transgressions, once they have become kālātīta (time-transcended), lose their 
social toxicity. The publication of the Dooshan Pañjī is the act of declaring 
all its records kālātīta — consigning them to history rather than allowing 
them to remain as suppressible secrets used to police social hierarchies.

The last word belongs to the document itself: no society anywhere has 
maintained such a comprehensive, honest record of human lineage — 
including its contradictions, transgressions, and impurities — across such a 
span of time, across such a breadth of population, under such conditions of 
adversity. That is the achievement of the Pañjī system, and the Dooshan 
Pañjī is its most honest chapter.

Chapter 19 
Dūṣaṇa Pañjī — The Black Book: A Complete Study 
from Genealogical Theories 
DŪṢAṆA PAÑJĪ / THE BLACK BOOK 
dūṣaṇa pañjī 
Gajendra Thakur | Videha Maithili Literature Movement 
A COMPLETE STUDY FROM THE PERSPECTIVE OF 
GENEALOGICAL THEORIES 
Methodology · Systems Analysis · Comparative Framework · Subaltern 
Dimensions 
*"Do not judge each day by the harvest you reap but by the seeds that 
you plant."* 
— Robert Louis Stevenson 
I. Introduction: The Pañjī System as Genealogical 
Architecture 
Gajendra Thakur's Dūṣaṇa Pañjī / The Black Book is not merely a historical 
compendium. It is the most sustained and rigorous engagement with a living 
genealogical system in South Asian scholarship — one that has recorded 
Maithila society's kinship networks without interruption for over 1, years. 
Published under the aegis of the Videha Maithili Literature Movement, this 
237-page work functions simultaneously as a primary source anthology, an 
institutional history, and a theoretical intervention in how genealogical 
knowledge is constituted, preserved, and deployed.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 388 
The document's subtitle, 'Genome Mapping: 450 A.D. to 2009 A.D.,' signals 
Thakur's intent: to read the Pañjī not as a pre-modern curiosity but as a proto-
scientific genealogical database that anticipates modern biological and 
demographic frameworks. This study examines the text from the full 
spectrum of genealogical theories — from classical kinship anthropology and 
biogenealogy to oral/documentary genealogy, subaltern genealogical 
critique, and digital archival theory. 
II. The Pañjī Typology: A Seven-Fold Genealogical System 
The Pañjī system is not monolithic. The text identifies seven distinct types, 
each serving a different genealogical function: 
1. Mūla Pañjī (Root Register) 
The foundational document tracing each family to its original settlement 
(mūlagrāma). The Mūla Pañjī embodies what genealogical theorists call 
'place-based lineage' — the encoding of identity through territorial origin. 
Before the reign of Maharaja Mādhava Siṃha of Darbhanga, all Pañjīs were 
structured uniformly as Mūla registers. Theoretically, this corresponds to 
Fortes's concept of the 'lineage span' — the territorial extent and generational 
depth over which kinship relations are tracked. 
2. Śākhā Pañjī (Branch Register) 
Commissioned by Maharaja Mādhava Siṃha (c. 1760 CE), the Śākhā Pañjī 
records the branching of families as they migrated from their origin villages. 
It captures what Pierre Bourdieu theorizes as 'social genealogy' — the 
documented legitimation of status as families dispersed across the Mithilā 
landscape. Thakur notes critically that the Śākhā Pañjī's introduction also 
inaugurated the hierarchical Śrotriya classification system, converting a 
scientific record into an instrument of social stratification. 
3. Gotra Pañjī (Lineage-Clan Register) 
This register maps the 20 gotra (patrilineal descent groups) and 167 mūlas 
(sub-clans) of Maithil Brahmin society. Genealogical theory classifies this as 
a 'segmentary lineage' system of the type analyzed by Evans-Pritchard among 
the Nuer: a hierarchical nesting of identity from the widest Vedic gotra 
(Śāṇḍilya, Vatsa, Kāśyapa, etc.) down to the specific village sub-clan.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 389 
4. Patra Pañjī (Leaf/Progeny Register) 
Dating from approximately 500 years before the text's composition, the Patra 
Pañjī records individual lineages (apatya) with their mūlagrāma. This 
corresponds precisely to what genealogists call 'vertical genealogy' — the 
diachronic tracing of a single line of descent across generations. 
5. Dūṣaṇa Pañjī (Defect/Blemish Register) 
The eponymous register of the work. The Dūṣaṇa Pañjī records instances of 
genealogical 'corruption' (kṣaraṇa) in a lineage: marriages outside prescribed 
boundaries, unknown parentage, cross-caste unions, and other departures 
from the normative system. Critically, the text argues that these records are 
disclosed only long after the events — after sufficient time has passed for the 
associated stigma to dissipate — serving as a genealogical corrective rather 
than a punitive instrument. 
6. Uteḍha (Ancestral Mapping Document) 
The Uteḍha is the operational tool of the Pañjī system: a genealogical matrix 
of 32 ancestors (sixteen on the bride's side, sixteen on the groom's side) 
constructed to determine marriage eligibility. It is the system's primary 
algorithm for sapiṇḍa prohibition — the prohibition of marriage between 
those sharing common ancestry. Theoretically, this is the closest analog in 
any world genealogical system to what modern genetics calls a 'kinship 
coefficient' calculation. 
7. Asvājanya-patra Pañjī / Siddhānta-patra (Certificate of Non-
Consanguinity) 
The formal written certificate issued by the Pañjīkāra (registrar) affirming 
that a proposed marriage does not violate sapiṇḍa rules. This document 
institutionalizes the genealogical system's social function: the transformation 
of archival genealogical data into actionable social certification. 
III. Institutional Genealogy: The Pañjī-Prabandha as State-
Sanctioned Archive 
From the perspective of institutional genealogical theory — concerned with 
how genealogical knowledge becomes formalized into state and social 
apparatus — the Pañjī-Prabandha represents a landmark case.
